name: Prompt Calibration for Realistic Essay Data Augmentation
population_or_context: Ninth-grade informative-explanatory writing assessment essays from a Midwestern U.S. state.
sample_size: 6,145 real essays sampled from about 40,000 examinees; 96 real essays selected across four score points; 288 simulated essays generated with three strategies.
methods: Three prompt engineering methods generated simulated essays from real same-score essays; expert raters blindly scored real and simulated essays and judged realism.
education_context: Statewide grade 9 writing assessment with a four-point scoring rubric focused on idea development and organization.
response_origin: Hybrid for evaluation: original student essays plus simulated essays generated from same-score student essays.
label_source: Operational professional rater scores for real essays; expert raters scored simulated essays and assigned real/simulated labels.
generation_model: Large language model; exact model version not extracted in this baseline pass.
generation_method: Prompt-engineered simulation using 25 Examples, Predict Next, and Sentence strategies.
prompt_strategy: 25 Examples contrasted real and prior GPT-generated examples; Predict Next inferred notes from one real essay to generate a second-like essay; Sentence rewrote each sentence while preserving error patterns.
evaluation_method: Compared expert-professional score agreement, QWK, average scores, and human real/simulated classification accuracy.
scorer_use: Synthetic-quality validation and calibration before using generated essays for automated scoring engine training augmentation.
real_response_comparator: Yes. Simulated essays were compared with matched real essays by score point and expert rater judgments.
sparsity_or_imbalance_motivation: The paper frames data augmentation as a way to mitigate limited training data and sparse observations at score points in automated scoring engine development.
synthetic_data_research_role: Synthetic essays were not used to validate a deployed scorer in this study; they were used to validate generation strategies for future scorer-training augmentation.
reported_metrics: Predict Next achieved agreement comparable to real essays; expert raters labeled only 25% of Predict Next simulations and 18% of 25 Examples simulations as simulated.
limitations: The study evaluates realism and score comparability rather than downstream scorer training; it uses one prompt, one grade level, and one state assessment context.
